args:
This is a 53 year-old African American male, with no significant past medical history who presented with a one-day history of right arm swelling. He woke up with a swollen arm and dull, aching pain in the right upper extremity. Our patient, a retired office manager, reported exercising daily over the prior week with a new “vibrating device” (Figure ). Patient denies any past interventions to the right upper extremity.
Patient denied: known malignancy, personal or family history of DVT, trauma, intravenous therapy, and drug abuse. Physical examination was unremarkable except for extensive right upper extremity swelling.
See Additional file .
Laboratory testing revealed CBC, CMP, PT/INR and aPTT all within normal limits.
A venous doppler study revealed an occlusive thrombus in the right axillary, proximal brachial and basilic veins. Access via a forearm vein was achieved and venography confirmed extensive occlusion of the right axillary, proximal brachial and basilic veins (Figure ).
An infusion catheter was positioned to allow tPA infusion of the thrombus throughout its entire length over the ensuing 24 hours. The following day, a venogram revealed the presence of a small residual thrombus and a 50% stricture in the right axillary vein (Figure ). Mechanical thrombectomy using an angiojet device, followed by balloon angioplasty and deployment of a 12 × 6 mm stent resolved the residual thrombosis and stenosis. Completion angiography showed complete resolution of the stricture and thrombus (Figure ).
The patient was discharged the following day on warfarin and aspirin. There were no adverse or unanticipated events.

- **Age**: 52 years old (1 points)

- **Troponin**: Initial values within normal range (0 points)

Therefore, the components of the HEART score are as follows: History (highly unlikely, 0), ECG (no findings, 0), Age (53 years old, 1), Risk Factors (no cardiovascular risk factors noted, 0), and Troponin (not indicated or normal, 0).

args:
This is a 47-year-old Caucasian male with a history significant for a living unrelated renal transplant two years prior to presentation and recent acute cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection. He presented with a two-day history of worsening, ascending lower extremity weakness, numbness, and tingling. The weakness had worsened rapidly to the point where he had difficulty ambulating and imbalance. Seven days prior to presentation, he had first presented with fever, chills, myalgias, and headache of one-week duration. Work-up at that time revealed acute CMV infection and he was started on oral valganciclovir at treatment doses. On review of systems, the patient complained of mild shortness of breath that worsened on exertion. He denied fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, lightheadedness, cough, lower extremity edema, or any changes in his urine output. He denied any recent travel, sick contacts, recent history of insect bites, or vaccinations.
His past medical history was significant for hypertension, dyslipidemia, asthma, and a distant history of melanoma. The etiology of his end stage kidney disease was biopsy proven focal segmental glomerulosclerosis presumed to be secondary to anabolic steroid use. He had a living unrelated kidney transplant two years prior to presentation. Induction was with steroids and basiliximab. His posttransplant course was complicated by biopsy proven BK Virus Nephropathy one year after transplant for which he was successfully treated with reduction in immunosuppression and intravenous immunoglobulin. At the time of transplantation, the donor was CMV positive, and the recipient was CMV negative. He received prophylaxis with valganciclovir for CMV more than one year. His current immunosuppression regimen consisted of prednisone 5 mg daily, tacrolimus 1.5 mg every twelve hours, and mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg every twelve hours.
On physical examination, the patient appeared anxious but was awake, alert, and oriented to person, place, and time. Vital signs included a blood pressure of 137/98 mmHg, pulse rate of 81/min, temperature of 97 degrees Fahrenheit, respiratory rate of 16 breaths/min, and a pulse oximetry of one hundred percent on room air. Physical examination was significant for a neurological examination, which demonstrated decreased motor strength in proximal and distal muscles in both upper and lower limbs (grades 3/5 and 2/5, resp.). He had absent deep tendon reflexes in his lower extremities. His gait could not be assessed due to lower extremity weakness and inability to ambulate. Two days before, his neurological exam was noncontributory except for decreased temperature sensation in his hands and feet.
Laboratory data from one week prior to presentation included mild thrombocytopenia, mild increase in liver transaminases, and a stable creatinine. Diagnosis of acute CMV infection was made based on a positive serum CMV IgM titer of 149 au/ml (normal < 30 au/ml), negative CMV IgG, and CMV PCR with 4800 copies/ml. Lumbar puncture and other serologic work-ups were negative. MRI of the brain done revealed no acute changes. Due to a diagnosis of acute CMV infection, he was started on oral valganciclovir. Laboratory data at this presentation included white blood cell count 5.9 k/microliter; hemoglobin 16.8 grams/deciliter; platelets 312 k/microliter; sodium 139 mmol/liter; potassium 3.6 mmol/liter; chloride 105 mmol/liter; bicarbonate 23 mmol/liter; blood urea nitrogen 17 mg/dl; and creatinine 1.28 mg/dl (at baseline). Urine heavy metal screen was negative for arsenic, cadmium, mercury, or lead. HIV, HTLV I/II, HBV, HCV, HSV, EBV, and HHV-6 PCR analyses were all negative. Lumbar puncture showed cerebrospinal fluid protein at 63 mg/dL, glucose of 68 mg/dL, cell count of 1, and albumin of 52.7 mg/dL. CMV PCR was positive in the CSF. Serum CMV PCR was 4000 copies/mL.
Based on positive serum and cerebrospinal fluid CMV PCR, the typical albuminocytologic dissociation of CSF analysis, and progressive neurological manifestations, a diagnosis of CMV associated Guillain-Barré Syndrome was made.
The patient was on an immunosuppression regimen that consisted of prednisone 5 mg daily, tacrolimus 1.5 mg every twelve hours, and mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg every twelve hours. The mycophenolate mofetil was discontinued, and the Prograf dosage was reduced to target FK506 troughs of 5–7. In addition to reduction in immunosuppression, the patient received eleven treatments of plasma exchange over a course of two weeks (each exchange consisted of two plasma volumes each with albumin as the replacement) and a total of 1 g/kg body weight of intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) in two divided doses. He was continued on valganciclovir 900 mg orally two times a day. After 72 hours, the patient began to improve with increased motor strength and improved negative inspiratory flow and vital capacity measurements. Every successive plasma exchange treatment seemed to subjectively show a favorable response to his motor recovery and muscle strength. After a two-week hospitalization, the patient regained his ability to ambulate and he was subsequently discharged on oral valganciclovir. He was continued on treatment doses of valganciclovir at 900 mg orally two times a day as an outpatient. His serum CMV PCR was negative after two weeks of treatment. One month later, the patient returned with lower extremity weakness and was readmitted for a presumed relapse. At this time, his CMV PCR was still negative. The patient was then treated with a total of 7 plasma exchanges. He regained his motor strength once again after initiation of plasma exchange and subjectively reported progressive improvement with each treatment. The patient had no adverse reactions to the IVIG or to the plasmapheresis. He has been in remission after treatment. His renal function has remained stable through all of this.

args:
A 66-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, as well as a recent diagnosis of Graves disease (positive thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins and radioactive iodine uptake of 99%) was transferred to our facility with a diagnosis of drug-induced liver injury with resultant acute liver failure. She had initially presented with several weeks of malaise after being started on methimazole for her Graves disease, and she had taken this medication for only 4 days. Initial laboratory findings included markedly elevated liver transaminases, and her international normalized ratio (INR) was also high (Figures and ). Although she was being assessed and stabilized in the emergency department, she developed rapid deterioration of her mental status with lethargy, weakness, and progressed to frank confusion and somnolence. This led to urgent endotracheal intubation to ensure airway protection. To evaluate her confusion, an magnetic resonance imaging of the brain was obtained which did not show any acute intracranial pathology but noted findings suggesting chronic small vessel ischemic disease and mild diffuse cerebral atrophy. Based on the findings of coagulopathy and progressive encephalopathy in the setting of acute liver injury, she was diagnosed as having acute liver failure, N-acetylcysteine was started, and she was transferred to our transplant center for evaluation for liver transplantation.
Her medications were reviewed thoroughly, and the possibility of liver injury secondary to methimazole was entertained, as this was a new medication, so it was initially discontinued. On evaluation, she was afebrile but in atrial fibrillation with a heart rate of 118. She was initially hypotensive at 86/49 mm Hg. She was believed to have a Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale of 55, thus highly suggestive of severe thyrotoxicosis/thyroid storm. A comprehensive etiological workup was undertaken which yielded a negative antinuclear antibody and anti-smooth muscle antibody; negative viral serology for hepatitis A, B, and C, human immunodeficiency virus, and herpes simplex virus. She had a normal creatine kinase, and her serum acetaminophen and salicylate levels were below detectable range. She underwent a urine toxicology screen that was negative for alcohol and other drugs. A complete abdominal ultrasound with Dopplers was notable for a grossly normal liver with mild steatosis but no structural or vascular abnormalities. A portable chest radiograph noted mild pulmonary edema and cardiomegaly. A transthoracic echocardiogram was obtained as well, and this was significant for a left ventricular ejection fraction of 36%, with an enlarged right ventricle, moderately reduced right ventricular function, and a dilated inferior vena cava. Her thyroid function tests revealed undetectable thyroid-stimulating hormone and elevated free thyroxine hormones (free T4 and T3) (Figure ). A diagnosis of thyrotoxicosis was made with resultant congestive cardiac failure and subsequent congestive hepatopathy. She was started on high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone and restarted on her methimazole. She improved clinically and had laboratory normalization of her INR and liver tests. She was ultimately discharged, clinically stable, to follow-up in the outpatient endocrinology clinic.

args: A 60-year-old man with a history of hypertension, variant angina, and hyperthyroidism developed high fever, cough, sputum, myalgia and dyspnea 4 days before admission. He was admitted to our hospital with a presumptive diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia and treated with empirical antibiotic therapy comprising piperacillin–tazobactam and levofloxacin (Fig. ). His systolic blood pressure was 100 mm Hg, heart rate 126 beats/min, respiratory rate 36/min, and body temperature 39 °C. The initial laboratory findings were as follows: WBC 14,500/mm3, platelets 237,000/mm3, CRP 302.6 mg/L, BUN 29.8 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.18 mg/dL. Liver function tests were within normal ranges. The initial O2 saturation was 85% and he received O2 (5 L/min) via facial mask. On day 3, his chest X-ray findings were worse and his oxygen requirement was increased. The patient was transferred to the ICU and treated with a non-invasive positive pressure ventilator. However, his severe hypoxemia was not improved (SaO2 = 94% with FiO2 100% and respiratory rate = 45/min), and he was intubated. On ICU day 2, his severe hypoxemia was not improved despite mechanical ventilation (i.e., PaO2/FiO2 = 67.6 with a PEEP of 15 cm H2O), and his heart rate increased to 151 beats/min (SAPS II, 36). We decided to apply VV ECMO, and started CRRT for fluid management. The initial ECMO settings were as follows: blood flow 5.0 L/min, sweep gas flow 4.5 L/min, and FiO2 1.0. On ECMO day 4, a PCR test for HAdV in a sputum sample taken at the time of admission was positive; based on this finding, the patient was administered cidofovir 5 mL/kg/week. The cell differential in the BAL fluid was as follows: neutrophils 43%, lymphocytes 34%, macrophage 22%, and eosinophils 1%. All tests of the BAL specimen for bacteria and viruses were negative. Although his blood gas oxygenation and chest X-ray showed an initial improvement during the early period of VV ECMO support, they were again worse on ECMO day 7 (Fig. ). Therefore, we started treatment with methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg q 24 h, which was then tapered slowly. We attempted to maintain a conservative fluid strategy (Table ) and the PaO2/FiO2 ratio and chest X-ray findings began to improve from ECMO day 9. On ECMO day 12, a tracheostomy was performed and ECMO and CRRT were removed on ECMO day 24. His net fluid balance was +10,219 mL (+425.8 mL/day) throughout the ECMO treatment (Fig. ). The patient was transferred to a general ward on ICU day 32 and finally discharged home. At his 1-year follow-up assessment, the patient was ambulatory and able to perform light housework.

args:
A 69-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with a severe headache and painful swelling in the right temporoparietal area. She also had a painless lump on her sternum. The lesion in the scalp had been growing during the past 4 months, and the lesion on the sternum had been present for the past 2 months. She denied any head or thorax trauma history. Her medical history was unremarkable except for warfarin therapy due to atrial fibrillation and hypertension. Her neurological examination was intact, and the infection markers were negative. The patient was referred to the neurosurgery outpatient clinic. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a lesion in the right temporoparietal area which caused local bone erosion. Together with the bone erosion, dural and scalp involvement were seen [].
Thorax and abdominal computed tomography was planned for a probable primary lesion, but there was not a suggestive focus except bone erosion on sternum just beneath the painless lump. Surgery was performed for diagnostic purposes for the skull pathology. The pathologic bone part was removed following the scalp incision. The dural area was also removed because the dura was also affected, and then, duraplasty was performed. The removed lesion was sent for histopathological examination. Since the diagnosis was not definite during the surgery, cranioplasty was planned after the confirmation of the diagnosis and treatment strategy. The pathology showed widespread strong positivity with CD20, bcl-2, bcl-6, and CD10, moderate positivity on the ground with CD5 and CD3 observed with a small number of T-lymphocytes. It was negative for CD56, thyroid transcription factor-1 and pancytokeratin were negative, and Ki-67 proliferation index was around 80% []. The specimen was diagnosed with high-grade diffuse large B-cell lymphoma []. The cerebrospinal fluid sample was not taken from the patient. Since the patient had no previous history of lymphoma, the patient was not considered bone marrow involvement before the histopathologic results. The patient was referred to a center with the oncology department. To the best of our knowledge, the patient is still under the treatment of the referenced oncology clinic. . The patient consent form was obtained.

args: A 24-year-old man with no known chronic medical problems, no history of prior hospitalizations, and no regular medications was air-lifted to the emergency department after being pulled from an icy mountain lake during a winter wilderness‐training exercise earlier the same afternoon; witnesses estimated that he was submerged for less than a minute but aspirated a significant amount of frigid water and then endured a two-hour sled evacuation through sub-zero ambient temperatures. Throughout transport he remained awake, conversant, and oriented, and the flight crew supplied low-flow supplemental oxygen through a Venturi mask that the respiratory therapist later confirmed to be delivering 30 % inspired oxygen; no intravenous fluids, vasopressors, sedatives, antibiotics, steroids, or analgesics were given in the field or en route. Upon stretcher arrival to the critical care bay—and before any hospital interventions—the initial set of vitals documented a core temperature of 35.8 °C measured rectally, a heart rate of 78 beats per minute in regular sinus rhythm, a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute with quiet chest excursions, and a non-invasive blood pressure of 118/72 mm Hg confirmed manually on the contralateral arm. The patient opened his eyes spontaneously, answered questions coherently, and obeyed commands without hesitation, yielding a full Glasgow Coma Score of 15. His skin was pale and cool to the touch but well perfused, with brisk capillary refill and no cyanosis or mottling noted; fingertips exhibited faint maceration consistent with prolonged damp exposure. Neck veins were flat at 30 degrees. Auscultation revealed a crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; distal pulses were strong and symmetric. Lung examination demonstrated fine inspiratory crackles scattered over both bases with slightly decreased breath sounds posteriorly, but no wheezes or stridor. The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender; bowel sounds were present. There was no costovertebral angle tenderness, no peripheral edema, and no focal neurologic deficit. A radial arterial blood sample obtained seven minutes after bay entry while he remained on the same 30 % oxygen source showed a pH of 7.41, PaCO₂ 38 mm Hg, and PaO₂ 59 mm Hg; end-tidal CO₂ on capnography was 36 mm Hg, corroborating adequate ventilation. Simultaneously drawn serum chemistry returned sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, glucose 96 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 12 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, indicating preserved renal function and normal electrolyte balance. Complete blood count demonstrated a white cell count of 9 000 cells/mm³ with a normal differential, hematocrit 43 %, hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL, and platelets 245 000/µL. Venous lactate measured at the bedside was 1.6 mmol/L. Coagulation profile showed an international normalized ratio of 1.0 and activated partial thromboplastin time of 31 seconds. Portable anteroposterior chest radiograph performed before any diuretics or antibiotics displayed faint bilateral perihilar opacities and increased interstitial markings without overt consolidation or pleural effusion, findings interpreted as early noncardiogenic pulmonary edema versus aspiration pneumonitis. Focused cardiac ultrasound revealed a small, vigorously contracting left ventricle and an inferior vena cava that collapsed more than 50 % with inspiration, suggesting euvolemia; no pericardial effusion was visualized. Lung ultrasound demonstrated scattered B-lines in both posterior lower zones, supporting the radiographic impression of mild interstitial involvement. Bedside abdominal scan showed normal solid organ echogenicity and no free fluid. Electrocardiography printed during the arterial puncture displayed normal sinus rhythm at 76 beats per minute, normal intervals, and no ST-segment deviations or Osborn waves. A Foley catheter placed for temperature monitoring produced 60 mL of clear yellow urine in the first fifteen minutes. No bronchodilators, antimicrobials, analgesics, warming blankets, or intravenous fluids had been started at the moment the intensive care team accepted him for close observation, passive rewarming, and serial reassessment of gas exchange; therefore the recorded values—temperature 35.8 °C, systolic pressure 118 mm Hg, diastolic pressure 72 mm Hg, pulse 78 beats per minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, arterial pH 7.41, serum sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, hematocrit 43 %, white blood cell count 9 000 /mm³, fraction of inspired oxygen 30 %, and PaO₂ 59 mm Hg—accurately depict his untreated physiologic state immediately upon presentation after a brief near-drowning event complicated by environmental hypothermia and mild aspiration-related hypoxemia.

args: A 72-year-old man with a background of long-standing coronary artery disease complicated by left-ventricular systolic dysfunction (last echocardiogram three months ago showed an ejection fraction of 35 %) and chronic stage 3 kidney disease arrived to the emergency department late this afternoon after three episodes of forceful vomiting, each containing streaks of bright red blood mixed with partially digested food, the largest estimated at roughly 100 mL. He reported that the first episode occurred shortly after lunch when a sudden wave of nausea and a sour metallic taste prompted him to lean over the kitchen sink; the retching continued twice more during the 20-minute ride to hospital in a private vehicle. He described dull, burning discomfort centered in the upper abdomen for the past week that was worse after his morning aspirin and after taking ibuprofen prescribed by his orthopedist for a recent flare of lumbar spinal stenosis. He denied black or tarry stools, denied seeing fresh blood in the toilet, and stated that his last bowel movement earlier today was medium brown and normally formed. He was able to ambulate to the car without needing to sit down and specifically denied any episodes of passing out, sudden loss of vision, or the need to grab support while walking. Cardiopulmonary history is notable for two hospital admissions in the past year for fluid overload requiring intravenous diuretics; at baseline he can climb one flight of stairs only if he pauses halfway, sleeps with two pillows to avoid shortness of breath, and takes carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg each morning, spironolactone 25 mg, and low-dose aspirin 81 mg. He has never been prescribed warfarin or direct oral anticoagulants. Other medical problems include hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 40 mg nightly and benign prostatic hyperplasia treated with tamsulosin. He quit smoking twenty years ago after a 25-pack-year history, drinks half a glass of wine with dinner on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. Family history reveals premature myocardial infarction in two older brothers; there is no history of gastrointestinal malignancy. Review of systems is negative for fever, chills, unintentional weight loss, night sweats, gingival bleeding, epistaxis, easy bruising, or recent dental procedures. On arrival to triage—before any intravenous access, antiemetics, proton-pump inhibitors, fluids, or blood products—the patient was seated upright, appeared anxious but conversant, and was clutching an emesis bag containing pink-tinged fluid. Vital signs recorded at that time were blood pressure 138/76 mm Hg using an automated cuff, heart rate 97 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 20 per minute, oral temperature 37.0 °C, and oxygen saturation 96 % breathing ambient air. He weighed 82 kg with a calculated body mass index of 27 kg/m². Conjunctiva were mildly pale, sclerae anicteric, and mucous membranes tacky. Jugular venous pulsation was observed at roughly 9 cm above the sternal angle with the head of bed at 45°, and there were faint bibasilar crackles on lung auscultation without wheezes. Cardiac examination revealed a displaced apical impulse and a soft third heart sound but no murmurs or pericardial rub. The abdomen was nondistended with mild tenderness to deep palpation in the epigastrium, no rebound, guarding, or palpable masses, and active bowel sounds. There was trace bilateral ankle edema; peripheral pulses were 2+ and symmetric, and capillary refill was under two seconds. Neurologic screening showed an alert, oriented individual with intact cranial nerves, full strength, and normal coordination. A digital rectal examination was deferred until after labs given the active emesis. Peripheral venipuncture obtained before any therapy demonstrated hemoglobin 13.0 g/dL, hematocrit 39 %, mean corpuscular volume 89 fL, platelet count 212 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 8.4 × 10⁹/L, blood urea nitrogen 34 mg/dL, serum creatinine 1.5 mg/dL (baseline 1.4), sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, and an international normalized ratio of 1.1. A 12-lead electrocardiogram performed at bedside showed normal sinus rhythm at 96–98 bpm with Q-wave in the inferior leads consistent with prior infarction but no acute ST-segment deviation. Portable chest radiograph revealed mild cardiomegaly and vascular congestion without infiltrate or pleural effusion. No medications or fluids had been administered at the time these findings and laboratory results were documented; two 16-gauge peripheral intravenous lines were nonetheless placed, a type and screen was sent, and the patient was instructed to remain nil per os while gastroenterology and cardiology consultations were requested for evaluation and management of this presumed upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage in the context of chronic ventricular dysfunction.

args:
An 88-year-old male presented to our emergency department (ED) with an acute onset of a painless loss of vision to his right eye. His past medical history was significant for hypertension, coronary artery disease and atrial fibrillation (on apixaban). He stated he was lying in bed reading when he had a sudden painless loss of vision to his right eye, described as a curtain closing, for which he presented to our ED ~24 h later. He denied any other symptoms including headache, scalp pain, jaw claudication, focal weakness, numbness, angina or shortness of breath.
Vitals on arrival to the ED were as follows: 36.9°C, heart rate of 62 beats/min, respiratory rate of 16 breaths/min, blood pressure of 123/53 mmHg and pulse oximetry of 99% on room air. His cardiopulmonary exam was unremarkable. His neurologic exam was notable for a National Institute of Health Stroke Scale score of +2 for complete unilateral vision loss to the right eye. Intraocular pressures were 14 mmHg bilaterally. His remaining physical exam was notable for a right afferent pupillary defect (APD). To further assess the eye and its structures, a point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) was then performed. Utilizing the high-frequency linear probe to the right eye, a transverse view demonstrated a retrobulbar spot sign (RBSS) in the distal portion of the central retinal artery within the optic nerve sheath (, red arrow). He had normal blood flow in his left eye (), and no blood flow was seen to the central retinal artery of the right eye, with the yellow arrow pointing to the RBSS (). The patient was then transferred to our tertiary referral center (TRC) for specialist consultation.
Ophthalmology visualized a plaque on the dilated fundoscopic exam with associated retinal ischemia. Given the onset of ~24 h prior to arrival, the risks of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) outweighed the benefits, as such, the patient was medically managed. His head computed tomography (CT) was unremarkable for acute changes. A magnetic resonance imaging MRI could not be done, secondary to his pacemaker not being compatible. His carotid duplex was without severe stenosis or thrombus. His echocardiography showed left ventricular diastolic dysfunction with an ejection fraction of 40%, which was similar to previous studies.
Overall, his workup was without evidence of carotid artery disease or vasculitis and it was suspected to be cardioembolic in origin related to his atrial fibrillation. On further questioning, it was found that the patient’s family member had passed away 2 weeks prior, leading to depression and multiple missed doses of his anticoagulation. Compliance of his anticoagulation was stressed on discharge. At his 1-month follow-up after his hospital admission, he has regained light perception and 15% of his vision.

args: A 54-year-old woman was evaluated late this morning in the medicine pre-operative clinic at the request of the hepatobiliary surgery service seven days before a planned open left hepatic lobectomy for a 6-cm segment II/III mass suspicious for fibrolamellar carcinoma; the operative note projects a midline and subcostal incision, Pringle maneuver–assisted parenchymal transection, an anticipated blood loss up to 600 mL, and a five-to-seven-day stay in the surgical intermediate care unit. She drove herself to the hospital, parked on level three, and walked the two city blocks from the garage to the clinic carrying a full purse without stopping; she reports doing most household chores, mowing a small lawn with a self-propelled mower, and climbing two flights of stairs to her bedroom every evening while holding a laundry basket, all without shortness of breath, ankle swelling, or need to pause for exertion. Sleep is obtained on a single pillow, she awakens easily refreshed, and she has not noticed nocturnal coughing or weight gain. Medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 18 years ago after routine screening revealed fasting hyperglycemia; oral agents maintained control for a decade, but she transitioned to insulin four years ago when carbohydrate counts became erratic during shift work. The regimen now consists of insulin glargine 28 units subcutaneously at bedtime with insulin lispro dosed according to a 1:12 carbohydrate ratio before meals and a correction factor of 1 unit for every 30 mg/dL above 150. Glucose logs recorded on her smart meter and reviewed in clinic last week show fasting readings mostly between 110 mg/dL and 155 mg/dL, occasional post-prandial excursions into the 220s, and no episodes of hypoglycemia requiring assistance. Her most recent hemoglobin A1c obtained by her endocrinologist six weeks ago was 7.5 %. Additional chronic conditions include well-controlled primary hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg every morning and hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine 88 µg daily; she takes a daily over-the-counter multivitamin and an occasional acetaminophen tablet for menstrual-related headache. She has never been diagnosed with congestive heart failure, and review of prior echocardiograms performed for routine hypertension follow-up have shown normal left ventricular function without valvular disease or reduced ejection fraction. There is no personal history of stroke, transient ischemic attack, carotid stenosis, or intracranial hemorrhage. There is no history of transient neurologic deficits, vision loss, or carotid intervention, and she has never been hospitalized for pulmonary or renal disorders. Surgical history consists of an uncomplicated laparoscopic salpingo-oophorectomy at age 46 for benign cystadenomas. She has never smoked, drinks a glass of red wine with dinner on weekends, and denies illicit drug use. The family pedigree reveals a mother with insulin-dependent diabetes diagnosed in her fifties and a father who underwent thoracic aortic aneurysm repair at 70; there is no early onset cerebrovascular or thrombotic disease among first-degree relatives. Review of systems today is negative for fever, night sweats, unintentional weight loss beyond 5 lb associated with pre-operative dietary counseling, chest pressure, palpitations, dyspnea on exertion, claudication, abdominal pain beyond the dull fullness under the left costal margin, melena, or easy bruising. After five minutes seated, vital signs in the right arm are blood pressure 126/74 mm Hg, regular pulse 72 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 98 % breathing ambient air, and oral temperature 36.8 °C; body mass index is 30 kg/m². She appears well nourished and in no distress. Jugular venous pulsation is not elevated at 45°, carotid upstrokes are brisk without bruit, the apical impulse is nondisplaced at the fifth intercostal space, and auscultation reveals crisp first and second heart sounds without S3, S4, murmurs, or pericardial rubs. Lungs are resonant to percussion with vesicular breath sounds bilaterally and no crackles or wheezes. Abdominal examination shows a palpable firm mass beneath the left lobe of the liver but no tenderness, rebound, or ascites; bowel sounds are present, and there is no splenomegaly. Extremities display warm skin, 2+ radial and dorsalis pedis pulses, and no pitting edema. Neurological screening discloses intact cranial nerves, symmetric 5/5 strength, preserved fine touch and proprioception, normal finger-to-nose testing, and a steady tandem gait. Pre-operative laboratory studies drawn an hour before the encounter demonstrate hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, white blood cell count 6.1 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 262 × 10⁹/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 0.87 mg/dL, yielding an estimated glomerular filtration rate exceeding 90 mL/min/1.73 m². Fasting plasma glucose in the same sample is 128 mg/dL; alanine aminotransferase is 48 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 52 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 180 U/L, total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, albumin 4.0 g/dL, and international normalized ratio 1.0. Twelve-lead electrocardiography obtained today shows normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats per minute with normal axis, intervals, and repolarization; there are no pathologic Q waves, ST-segment deviations, or T-wave inversions. Upright chest radiograph reveals clear lung fields and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. Airway evaluation by the anesthesia team documents a Mallampati class I view, inter-incisor distance of 4.5 cm, and full cervical extension without limitation. She has been advised to continue antihypertensive and thyroid medications up to and including the morning of surgery, administer 75 % of the usual basal insulin dose the night before, withhold rapid-acting insulin while nil per os unless peri-operative glucose exceeds 200 mg/dL, and restrict oral intake to clear liquids until two hours prior to arrival. Sequential compression devices, early ambulation, incentive spirometry, and tight blood glucose monitoring are planned post-operatively. After detailed discussion of potential hemorrhagic, infectious, respiratory, thrombotic, and metabolic complications, the patient expressed clear understanding of the surgical pathway and committed to comply with peri-operative instructions.

args: A 70-year-old woman who resides in her own bungalow and still gardens most mornings was brought to the emergency department by her husband shortly after lunchtime because of forty-eight hours of feverish chills, a productive cough yielding yellow-green sputum, and new right-sided chest discomfort that worsened whenever she tried to lie flat; she deliberately skipped her usual morning prescriptions—carvedilol, low-dose bumetanide, and nadolol prescribed for portal-related variceal prophylaxis—so that, in her words, “the numbers won’t be watered down.” She had eaten only dry toast at dawn and drank no coffee, alcohol, or acetaminophen before arrival, and she used neither her home incentive spirometer nor the salbutamol metered-dose inhaler she keeps for occasional winter wheeze. Past medical background includes longstanding congestive pump dysfunction following an anthracycline-based chemotherapy course in early adulthood for Hodgkin disease (she has had no malignant recurrence and has routine oncology surveillance that remains clear) and biopsy-confirmed non-alcoholic steatohepatitis with compensated portal hypertension; her last hepatology clinic visit three months earlier documented normal synthetic markers, occasional esophageal varices managed endoscopically, and no ascites. She has never required renal replacement therapy and her primary-care records show an estimated glomerular filtration rate consistently above 60 mL/min; she denies any history of stroke, transient neurologic events, carotid surgery, or focal deficits. Immunizations are current, including both pneumococcal formulations and this season’s influenza injection, and she quit a fifteen-pack-year cigarette habit nearly two decades ago. On ambulance roll-in she sat upright, conversed in full sentences, correctly recited the date and the names of her three grandchildren, and followed multi-step commands without hesitation. Skin was warm and lightly diaphoretic, sclerae were anicteric, and nail beds were pink with brisk capillary refill. First-pass, untreated vital signs obtained while she breathed ambient air at this sea-level facility were: oral temperature 38.05 °C, regular radial pulse 75 beats per minute confirmed on telemetry, non-invasive right-arm blood pressure 103/61 mm Hg verified manually, respiratory rate counted over a full minute at 20 breaths per minute with mild intercostal effort but no accessory muscle use, and fingertip pulse oximetry varying between 93 % and 94 %. Jugular venous contour was mildly elevated at the angle of Louis, similar to her cardiology baseline, and trace bilateral ankle edema was noted. Cardiac auscultation revealed an S3 gallop already documented in previous notes as well as crisp S1 and S2 without new murmurs or rubs. Pulmonary examination demonstrated dullness to percussion and absent breath sounds over the dependent half of the right posterior hemithorax with coarse inspiratory crackles and egophony just above that zone; the left lung transmitted vesicular airflow throughout. Abdomen was soft, non-tender, and mildly distended; a smooth liver edge was palpable 2 cm below the costal margin and no splenomegaly was appreciated. A focused neurologic screen showed equal, reactive pupils, intact extra-ocular movements, fluent speech, and 5/5 strength in all extremities. Capillary glucose checked immediately prior to phlebotomy read 93 mg/dL. Within ten minutes of bay entry—before any fluids, antimicrobials, vasodilators, or supplemental oxygen—peripheral venous blood and a right radial arterial sample were drawn. The basic metabolic panel returned sodium 140 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 104 mEq/L, bicarbonate 23 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 30 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. A complete blood count demonstrated leukocytes 15.1 × 10⁹/L with 88 % segmented neutrophils, hematocrit 33 %, hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, and platelets 189 × 10⁹/L. Arterial blood gas on room air revealed pH 7.36, PaCO₂ 37 mm Hg, PaO₂ 86 mm Hg, and calculated bicarbonate 21 mEq/L, consistent with mild hypoxemia and near-physiologic acid–base balance. Serum lactate was 1.7 mmol/L, and high-sensitivity troponin I fell below the analytical threshold. A portable postero-anterior chest radiograph obtained before any therapy demonstrated a dense consolidation within the posterior segment of the right lower lobe accompanied by a crescentic fluid collection layering laterally that blunted the ipsilateral costophrenic angle; cardiac silhouette matched prior films and there was cephalization of pulmonary vasculature similar to previous images. Electrocardiography displayed sinus rhythm at 74 beats per minute with low-voltage QRS complexes and nonspecific ST-T changes unchanged from tracings in her cardiology file. Two sets of peripheral blood cultures, a nurse-assisted expectorated sputum sample for Gram stain and culture, urinary antigens for Streptococcus pneumoniae and Legionella pneumophila, and a multiplex respiratory viral PCR panel were dispatched to the laboratory prior to any antibiotic, diuretic, vasodilator, bronchodilator, corticosteroid, or oxygen order. Up to the moment this narrative is completed she has received only the minimal heparinized saline needed to maintain patency of one 18-gauge peripheral catheter, ensuring that every physiologic observation, laboratory value, and imaging finding reported above represents her untreated state at presentation.

args: A 73-year-old man was evaluated this afternoon on the orthopedic surgery ward in preparation for a scheduled left total knee replacement that is set to take place tomorrow morning under general anesthesia and a femoral nerve block; the operative team anticipates a three-hour procedure with at least several days of inpatient recovery. The forthcoming joint reconstruction is the latest step in management of end-stage tricompartmental osteoarthritis that has limited his ability to stand for more than a few minutes over the past year. Three weeks ago he underwent a laparoscopic sigmoid resection for complicated diverticulitis at this same hospital, an operation that lasted just over four hours and required two postoperative nights on the surgical floor before discharge; he recovered uneventfully from that abdominal procedure. Unfortunately, one week after going home he slipped while descending his porch steps, landing awkwardly on his left leg and sustaining a minimally displaced lateral tibial plateau fracture confirmed by plain radiographs and CT scan. Because the articular surface remained congruent, the fracture was treated non-operatively with protected weight bearing in a hinged neoprene brace rather than a hard cast. Since that fall he has been largely confined to his bedroom recliner or bed, getting up only with assistance to use the bedside commode, and nursing documentation shows fewer than 50 feet of ambulation in the last four days; he notes that any attempt to move around causes immediate knee pain that he fears will worsen the fracture. He denies fevers, shakes, night sweats, purulent sputum, dysuria, or abdominal discomfort since the colectomy, and recalls no recent episodes of systemic infection. Review of systems is negative for orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, new ankle swelling, exertional chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or transient neurologic deficit. He has a long history of tobacco-related chronic bronchitis; although he quit cigarettes 10 years ago after a 45-pack-year exposure, he still uses a twice-daily long-acting muscarinic inhaler and keeps a rescue albuterol inhaler nearby for intermittent wheeze but has not required oral steroids or emergency visits this year. He has never experienced clot formation in the legs or lungs, and none of his close relatives has been treated for a thrombotic event. He recalls receiving unfractionated heparin only during prior surgeries without any unexplained drop in platelet counts, and no physician has ever advised him of an underlying blood-clotting disorder. Other chronic conditions include diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and well-managed hypertension; there is no personal history of cancer, inflammatory bowel illness, myocardial infarction, or peripheral vascular disease. Current medications are tiotropium, albuterol as needed, lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen-hydrocodone for pain, and a multivitamin; he takes no hormone therapy, steroids, anticoagulants, or anti-platelet agents other than baby aspirin, which was held yesterday per orthopedic protocol. Socially, he lives with his spouse in a single-story home, used to garden regularly, drinks a glass of wine on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. On examination he is resting supine in an adjustable hospital bed, conversant and oriented. Vital signs: temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 128/76 mm Hg, pulse 84/min and regular, respiratory rate 18/min, oxygen saturation 95 % on room air. He is slender with body mass index 23 kg/m² and breathes comfortably without accessory muscle use. Cardiovascular assessment reveals a normal S1 and S2 with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear to auscultation with slightly prolonged expiratory phase but no crackles or rhonchi. Abdomen is soft and non-tender; a well-healed 5-cm infra-umbilical trocar scar is noted without hernia or incisional pain. The left lower extremity is encased in a black hinged brace locked at 30 degrees; gentle palpation along the lateral tibial plateau produces mild discomfort but there is no warmth, erythema, or swelling, and calf circumference is symmetric to the right side. Skin of both legs is warm and well perfused, without discoloration, varicosities, or pitting edema. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ bilaterally. Sensory and motor testing show intact light touch and full strength of ankle dorsiflexion, though knee extension is limited by pain. There is no peripherally inserted central catheter or other indwelling vascular device; two 20-gauge peripheral IVs placed yesterday remain patent in the forearm. Neurologic examination is nonfocal. Laboratory results obtained two hours ago reveal hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 39 %, platelet count 247 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.3 × 10⁹/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, glucose 108 mg/dL, AST 22 U/L, ALT 24 U/L, and a baseline coagulation profile of PT 12.3 s (INR 1.0) and aPTT 30 s. Electrocardiogram records normal sinus rhythm at 82 bpm with nonspecific ST-T wave changes unchanged from previous tracings. Upright chest radiograph shows hyperinflated lungs consistent with his chronic bronchitis but no acute infiltrate or effusion. Pulmonary function tests from last year demonstrated an FEV₁ of 62 % predicted without significant bronchodilator response. The anesthesia service has deemed his airway Mallampati class II, and he tolerates lying flat without desaturation. He has had nothing by mouth since midnight, and no mechanical or pharmacologic thromboprophylaxis has yet been initiated as the peri-operative plan is still being finalized by the surgical team in conjunction with medicine and anesthesia.

args:
A 56-year-old man is reviewed in the short-stay pre-operative area an hour before transfer to the minimally invasive suite, where the surgical team plans a four-port laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy expected to last about ninety minutes under general anesthesia with overnight observation on the bariatric ward. He ambulated into the unit unassisted after parking his own car, reports that he still completes a two-mile neighborhood walk every evening with his spouse, and denies any recent limitation in daily activities other than occasional exertional dyspnea attributed to long-standing chronic obstructive pulmonary disease for which he uses once-daily tiotropium and intermittent albuterol; he has not required oral corticosteroids or hospital admission for a pulmonary exacerbation in more than two years, though he continues to carry a 35-pack-year smoking history and quit only three months ago. He has not developed pneumonia or other lower respiratory tract infection within the past month, and there has been no episode of sepsis or bacteremia requiring antimicrobials. The patient has never been treated for congestive heart failure, coronary ischemia, cerebrovascular events, spinal cord injury, or systemic infection, and he has avoided inpatient stays or operative procedures during the past twelve months apart from routine colonoscopy. Musculoskeletal history is free of casts, fractures of the pelvis or long bones, or multi-system trauma, and he currently sleeps in his own bed rather than a recliner without orthopnea. He recalls that his father developed a blood clot in the calf following hip arthroplasty in middle age and that a paternal aunt required long-term warfarin after an unprovoked pulmonary embolus; because of that background he underwent a thrombophilia work-up last year that demonstrated normal factor V Leiden genotype, normal prothrombin gene analysis, negative lupus anticoagulant, and a mild elevation of fasting plasma homocysteine at 18 μmol L⁻¹, for which lifestyle and vitamin supplementation were recommended. He himself has never experienced unilateral leg swelling, chest pain with breathlessness, or radiologist-confirmed venous thromboembolism, and duplex examinations performed twice in the past decade for vague calf aches were unremarkable. Examination today reveals serpentine, thin-walled superficial veins that become more prominent when his feet are placed dependent, coursing along the medial aspects of both lower legs without tenderness, edema, or skin discoloration; calf circumferences measured 10 cm below the tibial tuberosities are 36 cm bilaterally and equal. No central lines, ports, or tunneled catheters are in situ; the only vascular access is a single 18-gauge peripheral cannula in the right cephalic vein through which lactated Ringer’s is running at keep-open rate. He has never developed thrombocytopenia or antibody-mediated complications during prior brief exposures to unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin in the peri-procedural setting. He also denies any history of inflammatory bowel disease such as Crohn’s disease or ulcerative colitis. 

Vital signs ten minutes before review: temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 132/78 mm Hg, heart rate 84 beats min⁻¹ in regular rhythm, respiratory rate 16 min⁻¹ with a mild occasional expiratory wheeze, and oxygen saturation 95 % on room air. Airway evaluation discloses Mallampati class II anatomy, full dentition, three-finger thyromental distance, and unrestricted cervical extension. Cardiovascular examination notes a nondisplaced apical impulse and crisp S₁-S₂ without murmurs, gallops, or rubs; jugular venous contour is not elevated. Breath sounds are vesicular with scattered end-expiratory wheeze over both upper lobes but no rales or rhonchi. The abdomen is protuberant yet soft and nontender, without palpable hepatosplenomegaly or hernia defects other than the planned operative site markings. Distal pulses are 2+ in dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial arteries, capillary refill is brisk, and there is no pitting edema. Neurologic screen shows the patient to be alert, oriented, with 5/5 strength throughout, intact sensation to light touch, and steady tandem gait when asked to walk in the corridor. Morning laboratory tests obtained from the peripheral line reveal hemoglobin 14.0 g dL⁻¹, hematocrit 42 %, platelets 248 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, leukocytes 7.2 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, sodium 139 mmol L⁻¹, potassium 4.2 mmol L⁻¹, bicarbonate 25 mmol L⁻¹, creatinine 0.96 mg dL⁻¹, AST 22 U L⁻¹, ALT 24 U L⁻¹, total bilirubin 0.6 mg dL⁻¹, prothrombin time 12.1 s with an international normalized ratio of 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 30 s; fasting lipid profile and HbA1c earlier this month were within target ranges after recent weight loss efforts. A 12-lead electrocardiogram printed on arrival shows normal sinus rhythm at 80 beats min⁻¹, QRS duration 90 ms, no pathologic Q waves, and flat ST segments without dynamic changes compared with a tracing from his pulmonary clinic six months ago. Posterior-anterior chest radiography obtained yesterday displays mild upper-lobe hyperlucency consistent with emphysematous change but otherwise clear lung fields and an unremarkable cardiac silhouette. Body mass index measured at registration is 34 kg m⁻². He has been nil per os since midnight, removed dentures and jewelry, and last used his bronchodilator four hours ago. Sequential calf compression sleeves are wrapped but remain inactive until induction, and pharmacologic antithrombotic prophylaxis has been deferred until after the surgical pause. The anesthesia team has discussed risks of venous stasis, pulmonary complications related to pneumoperitoneum, and the importance of early ambulation and incentive spirometry post-operatively; the patient verbalizes understanding and consent, requests no further clarification, and awaits transfer to the operating theater.

args:
A 68-year-old man present at hospital after a sudden onset of visual disturbances, dizziness and slight headache. Neurological examination 25 min later revealed right-sided homonymous hemianopsia and marginal evidence of non-fluent aphasia from a stroke 20 months earlier. Brain MRI revealed restricted diffusion on diffusion-weighted imaging in the medial part of the left occipital lobe without demarcation in FLAIR.
The patient’s medical history included recurrent transient ischemic attacks. He had been taking dabigatran 110 mg b.i.d. for NVAF. At the time of treatment initiation, his renal function was slightly impaired; however, the prescribed dose regimen may have been inappropriate given the patient’s age and medical history. Laboratory examinations revealed an eGFR of 102 mL/min, a dTT of 34 ng/mL and an aPTT of 34 s. It was decided to neutralize the anticoagulant activity of dabigatran and to perform thrombolysis with rt-PA, considering hemianopsia as a functionally relevant deficit (mRS of 3).
The patient received a rapid i.v infusion of idarucizumab (5 g), which was followed, after 10 min (and a total of 110 min from symptom onset), by intravenous thrombolysis with rt-PA (70 mg). Transcranial Doppler sonography shortly after rt-PA infusion showed regular blood flow in both posterior cerebral arteries. Transthoracic echocardiography on day 2 did not reveal cardiac thrombi or structural heart disease. The patient was discharged on day 3 with slightly improved hemianopsia (NIHSS 3, mRS 2). Dabigatran treatment was resumed at the higher dosage (150 mg b.i.d.) as renal function tests were normal.

args:
A previously healthy, 61-year-old right-handed woman without history of epilepsy was transferred to our hospital for further evaluation and treatment of hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, confusion, and a brief seizure. Initial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a 3.5 × 3.3 × 2.8 cm right frontotemporal meningioma with an accompanying midline shift of 3 mm (Fig. ).
Upon arrival, her neurological exam was notable for confusion and otherwise was nonfocal. Two clinical seizures with semiology of lip smacking, left head and eye deviation, and altered awareness were also observed. Both seizures ceased following intravenous lorazepam 2 mg without any improvement in mental status. Electroencephalography (EEG) revealed two right temporoparietal seizures with frequent T8/P4 sharp waves. She was treated with intravenous fosphenytoin 15 mg/kg. Continuous video-EEG monitoring showed nonconvulsive SE (NCSE). The patient was intubated and admitted to the neuro-intensive care unit. Stepwise treatment with propofol, midazolam, and ketamine was started, with burst suppression achieved in less than 24 h. Her antiepileptic drug (AED) management consisted of levetiracetam, lacosamide, and phenobarbital. Unfortunately NCSE recurred each time with weaning of general anesthesia. Three days after admission, she underwent resection of the right frontotemporal mass which was consistent with a WHO grade I meningioma (Fig. ). During her hospital stay, she was also treated for hyperparathyroidism secondary to a parathyroid adenoma that was treated medically and then surgically. Postoperatively, she remained seizure free, but her mental status did not improve. Intermittent EEGs showed diffuse slowing with focal right hemispheric arrhythmic delta slowing and continued right T8/P4 sharp waves. She was discharged to a long-term care facility in a minimally responsive state with tracheostomy and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy. Her mental status improved after discharge, but she had persistent generalized weakness due to critical illness myopathy from her prolonged intensive care unit stay. At 2-month follow-up, she had normal memory, attention, and language functions.
Two weeks later, she presented with confusion and left face twitching. EEG showed continuous right hemispheric spike and wave activity consistent with focal NCSE. Her preadmission AEDs were continued with the addition of valproic acid without subsequent improvement. EEG burst suppression was achieved with propofol, ketamine, midazolam, and pentobarbital. Brain MRI showed right superior temporal encephalomalacia and gliosis (Fig. ). NCSE emerged each time anesthetic medication was lowered despite four antiepileptic medications. Due to her previous SRSE with prolonged recovery phase and current poor prognosis, the family agreed for recurrent surgical treatment. She underwent tailored right temporal lobectomy 8 days after admission with removal of the superior, middle, and inferior temporal gyri (6 cm from the tip). Hippocampectomy was guided by intraoperative electrocorticography with resultant cessation of epileptiform activity along the margin of the resections (Fig. ). Postoperative EEG continued to show frequent right posterior temporal periodic epileptiform discharges for another 15 days without clinical improvement. Topiramate was added to her AED regimen, and propofol and midazolam was again titrated to EEG burst suppression for 2 days. As general anesthesia was weaned, the EEG slowly improved to diffuse theta and delta range slowing. Surgical pathology showed nonspecific leptomeningeal necrotizing inflammation in medium-sized arteries, focal cortical neuronal loss, and gliosis. The patient received high-dose fosphenytoin steroids for 5 days for possible autoimmune encephalitis. Tests for autoimmune markers and paraneoplastic antibodies as well as imaging studies for neoplasia in any other part of the body were negative except for a nonspecific elevated antinuclear antibody. She was transferred to a long-term care facility on topiramate 400 mg/day, valproic acid 3,000 mg/day, phenobarbital 300 mg/day, lacosamide 400 mg/day, and levetiracetam 5,000 mg/day.
At 9-month follow-up, neuropsychological testing could not be performed due to somnolence and lack of cooperation. Over 3 years the patient gradually improved her interaction and alertness, with concomitant decrease in her AEDs. As her cognitive status improved, a clinical picture of sensory aphasia was revealed. This manifested as relatively fluent and articulate speech with notable paraphasic errors, verbal perseveration, word salad, and alexia. Neuropsychological testing 34 months postoperatively could not be completed due to her dense sensory aphasia. The Mississippi Aphasia Screening Test showed an improving Wernicke-type aphasia that was evolving into a transcortical sensory aphasia profile with relatively fluent and articulate speech, paraphasic errors, poor comprehension, inconsistent response to questions and command following, restricted repetition of single words only, agraphia, and inability to read complex sentences.

args: A 52-year-old man with a history of alcohol-associated cirrhosis complicated by prior episodes of ascites and grade II encephalopathy was brought to the emergency department by ambulance after being found somnolent and jaundiced on the floor of his apartment by a neighbor who had last seen him well two days earlier. According to pre-hospital personnel, he was moaning incomprehensibly, opened his eyes only to painful sternal rub, and withdrew his upper limbs sluggishly, prompting them to perform a more formal neurologic check that yielded a Glasgow Coma Score of 7 in the field; no sedatives, opioids, or paralytics had been given during transport, and the airway remained uninstrumented throughout. On arrival he lay in the resuscitation bay breathing through a non-rebreather mask connected to wall oxygen; an arterial blood gas drawn within ten minutes demonstrated a PaO₂ of 65 mm Hg while receiving a measured 52 % inspired oxygen fraction, pH 7.31, and PaCO₂ 48 mm Hg. Respiratory rate was 28 breaths/min with see-saw pattern but no accessory muscle fatigue, and auscultation revealed coarse crackles at both lung bases without wheeze. His skin was cool and slightly clammy, capillary refill prolonged to four seconds, and automated cuff readings repeated three times showed a systolic pressure of 80 mm Hg and diastolic pressure of 45 mm Hg before any crystalloid infusion or vasoactive medications were started; heart rate was 118 beats/min in a regular rhythm, and peripheral pulses were thready. Scleral icterus and spider angiomas were evident, the abdomen was distended with a positive fluid wave yet soft to palpation, and shifting dullness suggested moderate ascites; there was right upper-quadrant tenderness without guarding. Lower extremities displayed pitting edema to the mid-shin. No stigmata of acute gastrointestinal bleeding were obvious, though dark dried emesis coated his beard, raising concern for recent hematemesis; nasogastric lavage performed later returned coffee-ground fluid but, at the time vital data were collected, no lavage had yet been attempted. Initial laboratory investigations obtained prior to any antibiotics, fluids, albumin, or lactulose included a complete blood count showing a white cell count of 14.1 × 10³/µL, hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL, and a platelet count of 115 000 /µL. Basic metabolic panel revealed sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, chloride 97 mmol/L, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, glucose 102 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 28 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, comparable to his documented outpatient baseline of 1.0. Total bilirubin was markedly elevated at 3.5 mg/dL with a direct fraction predominance, aspartate aminotransferase 88 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 60 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 190 U/L, in keeping with cholestatic decompensation. International normalized ratio was 2.1 and activated partial thromboplastin time 46 seconds, reflecting synthetic dysfunction. Venous lactate returned at 3.8 mmol/L. Point-of-care ultrasound demonstrated a small, hyperdynamic left ventricle, a flattened inferior vena cava with >50 % collapse on inspiration, and moderate ascitic fluid without sonographic evidence of portal vein thrombosis. Bedside chest radiograph obtained while he remained on the mask showed bilateral hazy opacities worse at the dependent zones, suggestive of aspiration pneumonitis or evolving pulmonary edema; cardiac silhouette size was normal. Foley catheterization performed on arrival drained scant amber urine, but review of the emergency medical services flow sheet combined with collateral history from a visiting friend suggested he had voided roughly 1.1 liters in the preceding twenty-four hours, consistent with preserved renal output. An electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes, and bedside glucose remained stable during observation. The patient had not received diuretics, beta-blockers, antibiotics, lactulose, intravenous albumin, proton pump inhibitors, pressors, or supplemental vitamin K before the numbers cited above were documented. Past surgical history was limited to laparoscopic umbilical hernia repair; he was unemployed, lived alone, and had no insurance. He reportedly drank a half-liter of vodka daily until three months earlier when he attempted to reduce intake, though empty bottles found at the scene suggested recent relapse. He smoked one pack per day for twenty years but quit five years ago, and he denied illicit drug use. Immunizations were unknown. There was no family history of liver disease. At bedside he appeared profoundly lethargic, unable to protect airway secretions though still breathing spontaneously, and coated with yellow sweat; bilateral palmar erythema and digital clubbing were noted. Intra-abdominal paracentesis, empiric broad-spectrum antimicrobials, and cautious volume resuscitation with crystalloids followed by albumin were being planned at the moment the above physiologic data were captured, but no therapeutic interventions had yet been initiated.

args: A 68-year-old man arrived this afternoon to the comprehensive peri-operative clinic at the request of the vascular surgery service, six days before a planned open repair of a 5.8-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm discovered incidentally on surveillance ultrasound for renal insufficiency; the operative team anticipates a transperitoneal midline approach lasting four to five hours with need for aortic cross-clamping below the renal arteries, possible cell-saver autotransfusion, and immediate postoperative admission to the surgical intensive care unit for invasive hemodynamic and urine-output monitoring. He strolled slowly from the parking lot using the handrail on the front steps and requested a brief rest in the waiting area, explaining that over the past year he has become short of breath after walking two New-York-City blocks or carrying a basket of laundry up one flight. Three years ago he was hospitalized for rapid weight gain, bilateral ankle swelling, and nocturnal breathlessness; echocardiography obtained during that admission demonstrated a globally dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 30 %, mild functional mitral regurgitation, and normal right-sided chamber size. He required two additional admissions for volume overload in the subsequent 18 months, most recently three months ago after a long road trip that limited his access to prescribed medications; intravenous loop diuretic therapy achieved a 4-kg net weight reduction. His current regimen includes carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, sacubitril-valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg with breakfast, spironolactone 25 mg at bedtime, and a 2-gram-sodium diet; he records daily morning weights in a notebook reviewed today and calls his primary clinician if he gains more than 2 lb in 24 hours. He sleeps on three pillows, awakens once or twice weekly gasping for air until he sits at the edge of the bed, and notes that his socks leave imprints by evening although the swelling subsides overnight. There have been no episodes of syncope, palpitations, or unexplained presyncope. Blood pressure has been elevated since his early fifties and is treated with amlodipine 10 mg each morning; type 2 diabetes diagnosed five years earlier is managed solely with metformin extended-release 1000 mg with supper and a carbohydrate-restricted diet taught by a nutritionist, with a glycated hemoglobin of 7.0 % at last month’s primary-care visit. He monitors capillary glucose twice weekly, reporting readings between 110 and 160 mg/dL and no hypoglycemic events. He has never experienced abrupt unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, monocular vision loss, or episodes suggestive of transient neurologic ischemia. Chronic kidney disease stage 3a, attributed to combined hypertensive and diabetic microvascular change, is co-managed with nephrology; serum creatinine has hovered in the 1.2–1.4 mg/dL range for the past two years. Surgical history is limited to a remote laparoscopic cholecystectomy without complications. He quit cigarette smoking 15 years ago after a 25-pack-year exposure, drinks two light beers on weekend evenings, and denies recreational drug use. Review of systems is negative for fever, chills, productive cough, abdominal pain, claudication, unexplained weight loss, or bleeding tendencies. After five minutes of quiet sitting, vitals are temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 114/70 mm Hg in the right arm, regular pulse 62 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 95 % on ambient air; body mass index is 29 kg/m². He appears comfortable at rest but breathes more heavily when turning to remove his coat. Jugular venous pulsation is visualized 5 cm above the sternal angle with the head of the bed at 45 °, carotid upstrokes are brisk without bruit, and the point of maximal impulse is laterally displaced in the sixth intercostal space. Cardiac auscultation reveals a quiet S1 and S2, a soft S3 gallop, and a grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur at the apex consistent with functional regurgitation; no pericardial rub is present. Lungs are clear in the upper fields but have fine late-inspiratory crackles at both bases. The abdomen is soft and nontender with a palpable pulsatile mass above the umbilicus, no guarding, and intact midline scar tissue from prior surgery. Extremities exhibit bilateral 1+ pitting edema to mid-shin, warm skin, and symmetric dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses. Neurologic examination shows full orientation, intact cranial nerves, 5/5 strength proximally and distally, normal sensation, and a steady narrow-based gait when he walks unassisted down the hallway. Pre-operative laboratory studies drawn one hour before the encounter demonstrate hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, hematocrit 38 %, platelet count 232 × 10⁹/L, white blood cell count 6.4 × 10⁹/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 26 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.26 mg/dL, corresponding to an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 56 mL/min/1.73 m²; fasting plasma glucose in the same panel is 132 mg/dL, and liver transaminases as well as international normalized ratio are within reference limits. A resting 12-lead electrocardiogram obtained in clinic shows sinus rhythm at 60 beats/min with low-voltage QRS complexes and nonspecific ST-T changes unchanged from a tracing four months ago. Upright posterior–anterior chest radiograph reveals cardiomegaly, mild pulmonary vascular congestion, and an enlarged descending aorta without pleural effusion. Airway assessment by anesthesia documents a Mallampati class II oropharynx, inter-incisor distance of 4 cm, thyromental distance of 6 cm, and full cervical extension. The anesthesiology team plans balanced general anesthesia with lumbar epidural catheter for postoperative analgesia, continuation of beta-blocker and renin-angiotensin–neprilysin inhibitor therapy through the morning of surgery, omitting the loop diuretic on the day of operation to avoid intravascular depletion, insertion of a radial arterial line and large-bore central venous catheter, and goal-directed crystalloid guided by stroke-volume variation. Sequential compression devices, subcutaneous low-dose heparin beginning the evening after surgery, incentive spirometry, and early ambulation have been reviewed; the patient verbalized understanding of peri-operative instructions, demonstrated accurate teach-back regarding medication timing and fasting, and expressed determination to proceed with definitive aneurysm repair to prevent rupture.

args:
A 29-years-old female experienced an episode of mild upper respiratory tract infection followed by a slowly developing dysphagia and dyspnea. This led to impaired exercise tolerance lasting several months, with recent subacute exacerbation. There was no significant history of travel or exposure to infectious agents. She had not suffered from fever or other acute or chronic infectious symptoms. Her palatine tonsils had been removed in childhood. She was diagnosed with epilepsy at age 17 and had been free of epileptic symptoms for over 5 years with levetiracetam (500 mg two times per day) and lamotrigine (150 mg two times per day). Her family history was unremarkable.
She spoke with hoarse voice, without signs or findings that would have suggested systemic involvement. There were no signs of generalized mucosal disease. Fiberoptic examination showed swelling of the lingual tonsil, epiglottic and arytenoid mucosa, causing airway obstruction (Fig. a). C-reactive protein concentration and blood sedimentation rate were low, and anti-nuclear, anti-neutrophil, anti-glomerular basement membrane, anti-myeloperoxidase, anti-proteinase 3, tissue transglutaminase and cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies were negative. Thyroid function was normal and thyroid peroxidase antibodies were 34 IU/ml (normal value < 60 IU/ml). Plasma parathyroid hormone (39 ng/l; normal 18–80 ng/l) and serum vitamin D-25 (77 nmol/l; normal > 50 nmol/l) were normal. No evidence for acute or chronic viral, bacterial, mycobacterial or fungal infections, including hepatitis B and C, human immunodeficiency virus and tularemia, was found.
Due to her swollen lingual tonsil and laryngeal mucosa causing airway obstruction, she was hospitalized and received empiric cefuroxime (1.5 g thrice daily) and methylprednisolone (75 mg once daily, height 173 cm, weight 63 kg, prednisolone dose 1.2 mg/kg) intravenously. After a transient positive response, she was discharged from the hospital after 6 days. Soon after, she was readmitted to due to a rapid reoccurrence of the symptoms. A modest positive response was seen after per oral clindamycin and oral prednisolone (60 mg/days) with dose reduction for 14 days. Lingual tonsil and epiglottis were biopsied. Histology showed granulomatous reaction in lingual tonsil with CD68+ (Kp-1) epithelioid histiocytes (Fig. ). The CD20+ B lymphocyte count was increased while Pax-5+ positive B lymphocyte count was normal. CD138+ plasma cells were scarce, and they showed polytypic kappa- and lambda light chain expression. The number of CD3+ T cells was normal, and most T cells were CD4+. Eosinophils were practically absent and well-formed follicles were not seen. Epstein–Barr virus in situ hybridization (EBER), p16, cytomegalovirus and mycobacteria were negative. Morphology, immunohistochemical staining, serum electrophoresis and bone marrow aspirate did not raise suspicion of lymphoid neoplasia, thus, no clonality studies were done.
Due to granulomas, sarcoidosis was considered. However, chest X-ray, computerized tomography of chest and ultrasound of neck and abdomen were unremarkable. Positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) showed increased fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake in the lingual tonsil, without evidence of widespread sarcoidosis (Fig. ). No other pathological FDG foci were found. Focal physiological FDG accumulation was seen in ureters on both sides of the spinal column. This is a common finding due to ureteral peristalsis and pooling of radiotracer in the recumbent patient. The patient had no neurological symptoms and brain magnetic resonance imaging was normal. Serum angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) was within normal range (11–20 U/l; reference range 9–65 U/l) while daily urine calcium output was normal or slightly elevated (5.0–6.31 mmol; reference range 1.2–5.5 mmol).
Subsequently, an unusual presentation of primary or secondary immunodeficiency was considered. There was no consanguinity; the patient or her family had no history of repeated infections or autoimmunity. White cell, lymphocyte, and B, T and NK lymphocyte subset counts were within normal limits. CD19+ B cell count was 153 × 109/l (normal range 80–616 × 109/l). Proportions of memory CD27+ B-cells (30.3%) and IgD−IgM−CD27+ switched memory B cells 7.2%; (normal range 6.5–29.2%) were normal and the percentage of activated CD21low B cells was high (6.6%; normal range 0.6–3.5%). Percentages of various CD3+CD4+ and CD3+CD8+ T cell subsets appeared normal. Serum total IgG (3.5 g/l; normal range 6.77–15.0 g/l) and all IgG subclass concentrations were low: IgG1 2.45 g/l (normal range 4.9–11.4 g/l), IgG2 0.91 g/l (normal range 1.5–6.4 g/l), IgG3 0.18 g/l (normal range 0.2–1.1 g/l) and IgG4 0.02 g/L (normal range 0.08–1.4 g/l). Anti-polysaccharide response to Pneumovax® were impaired, with only 3/10 serotypes reaching a level of 0.35 µg/ml after immunization. However, serum IgA (0.90 g/l; normal range 0.52–4.02 g/l) and IgM concentrations (0.57 g/l; normal range 0.47–2.84 g/l) were normal. Dihydrorhodamine test excluded chronic granulomatous disease. These findings were thus consistent with either granulomatous CVID or most likely secondary drug-induced hypogammaglobulinemia with a novel clinical presentation [–]. Genetic analysis (Blueprint Genetics, Primary Immunodeficiency Plus panel. ) did not find known mutations causative of CVID-like or other primary immunodeficiencies.
Due to symptomatic upper airway obstruction, dysphagia, lack of evident infection and unsatisfactory response to prednisolone treatment and possible granulomatous CVID, the patient received immunoglobulin replacement and rituximab therapy (Mabthera® 100 mg, 200 mg and 500 mg on three consecutive days combined with 100 mg hydrocortisone and 1 g paracetamol), with a favorable response within 2 weeks accompanied by significant improvement in dysphagia and respiratory symptoms. Seven months later, her exercise capacity was good. She has remained asymptomatic for over 16 months after rituximab treatment. Her fiberoptic pharyngeal findings improved (Fig. ). She continues to receive subcutaneous immunoglobulin replacement with serum IgG levels in the range of 10 to 12 g/l.
