args: A 58-year-old woman with a history of diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, mild osteoarthritis of the knees, and remote cholecystectomy presented to the emergency department 90 minutes after the abrupt onset of gnawing epigastric pain radiating straight through to the back while she was cleaning the breakfast dishes; the discomfort was accompanied by two bouts of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis and a single syncopal episode witnessed by her spouse. No ambulance services were involved because she insisted on being driven directly to the hospital, and en route she received neither intravenous fluid nor analgesia—only a lightweight nasal cannula set at 1 L min⁻¹ that the triage nurse later verified on the wall flowmeter. She had taken her routine morning medicines—metformin 500 mg and a multivitamin—four hours earlier, denied alcohol use or new medications, and reported 2.1 L of water intake since waking, recorded in a smartphone hydration app she uses for weight management. On arrival, before any emergency department therapy could be instituted, she sat upright on the stretcher, alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation, conversing in full sentences without dyspnea. Initial vital signs obtained by acuity nursing showed temperature 37.2 °C, heart rate 96 beats min⁻¹ in regular rhythm, respiratory rate 18 breaths min⁻¹, and an automated cuff reading of 119 / 67 mm Hg that was confirmed two minutes later via a flushed right radial arterial line placed for anticipated serial blood gases; mean arterial pressure calculated from that tracing was 84 mm Hg. Capillary refill was less than two seconds, mucous membranes were moist, and skin turgor was preserved. Lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally with equal chest expansion, and she generated tidal volumes comfortably without accessory-muscle use; the ventilatory pattern remained entirely spontaneous, and no positive-pressure device or advanced airway was employed at any time. The arterial catheter, sited prior to any crystalloid administration, immediately yielded a specimen analyzed within the resuscitation bay: pH 7.36, PaCO₂ 35 mm Hg, HCO₃⁻ 19 mEq L⁻¹, lactate 1.9 mmol L⁻¹, and PaO₂ 119 mm Hg while the oxygen analyzer attached to the flowmeter confirmed an inspired fraction of 0.24 delivered through the nasal prongs. Cardiovascular examination revealed normal S₁ and S₂ without murmurs, jugular venous pulsation at 4 cm above the sternal angle, and warm extremities. Abdominal inspection showed mild epigastric distension; palpation elicited marked tenderness in that region with voluntary guarding but no rebound, and bowel sounds were present though hypoactive. There was no scleral icterus, spider angioma, or ascites. Neurologic evaluation demonstrated pupils 3 mm briskly reactive, intact extra-ocular movements, full-strength symmetric limb power, and appropriate responses to questions, resulting in a Glasgow Coma Score of 15. A 16-French Foley catheter inserted as part of the initial survey drained 110 mL of clear straw-coloured urine immediately, and her smartwatch-linked urine tracker displayed cumulative output of 1 520 mL over the preceding twenty-four hours, suggesting adequate renal perfusion. Blood drawn concurrently with the arterial sample—prior to any opiate, anti-emetic, proton-pump inhibitor, or intravenous fluid—returned sodium 137 mmol L⁻¹, potassium 4.3 mmol L⁻¹, chloride 101 mmol L⁻¹, glucose 148 mg dL⁻¹, blood urea nitrogen 18 mg dL⁻¹, and serum creatinine 1.05 mg dL⁻¹, matching her last primary-care baseline. Total bilirubin measured 1.12 mg dL⁻¹ with predominantly indirect fraction; aspartate aminotransferase 42 U L⁻¹, alanine aminotransferase 39 U L⁻¹, alkaline phosphatase 128 U L⁻¹, and international normalized ratio 1.0 were also reported. Complete blood count showed hemoglobin 13.1 g dL⁻¹, hematocrit 38 %, leukocytes 11.8 × 10³ µL⁻¹ with 6 % band forms, and a platelet concentration of 186 000 µL⁻¹. Serum amylase and lipase were markedly elevated at 686 U L⁻¹ and 1 132 U L⁻¹, respectively, supporting the clinical suspicion of acute pancreatitis, while high-sensitivity troponin was below detectable threshold. Bedside abdominal ultrasound performed before any resuscitative fluid revealed an edematous pancreas without gallstones and a collapsible inferior vena cava measuring 1.7 cm that narrowed more than 50 % on inspiration, findings consistent with euvolemic status. Upright chest radiograph, obtained while she remained on the low-flow nasal cannula, displayed clear lung fields without pleural effusion or free subdiaphragmatic air. Electrocardiography documented normal sinus rhythm at 94 beats min⁻¹ with nonspecific ST-segment flattening and a corrected QT of 436 ms. Up to the precise moment all physiologic and laboratory data were captured, the patient had received no narcotic, anti-secretory drug, antibiotic, vasopressor, or intravenous crystalloid; she breathed comfortably on 24 % inspired oxygen achieving an arterial oxygen tension of 119 mm Hg, maintained a blood pressure of 119 / 67 mm Hg without pharmacologic support, exhibited a platelet count of 186 000 µL⁻¹, displayed total bilirubin of 1.12 mg dL⁻¹, preserved renal function with serum creatinine 1.05 mg dL⁻¹ and documented urine production of 1 520 mL in the prior day, and remained fully alert with a Glasgow Coma Score of 15 despite significant epigastric pain likely attributable to new-onset gallstone-independent pancreatitis.

args: A 67-years-old Caucasian man presented for refashioning of a left below-knee amputation stump. His co-morbidities included diabetes, cardiovascular disease, peripheral neuropathy, obesity and asthma. Medications included insulin, amlodipine, lisinopril, gabapentin and salbutamol. Previous regional block and general anaesthesia were uneventful. The patient made an informed choice of spinal anaesthesia. Standard perioperative monitoring was used. Spinal block was performed aseptically at the L4/5 spinal space with a 25 G Whitacre needle and 2.5 ml of heavy 0.5% bupivacaine plus diamorphine 300 mcg. The spinal block procedure was uneventful. Adequate motor and sensory block was achieved at five minutes. Adequate sensory block up to the T8 dermatome was confirmed by loss of cold sensation. The intraoperative course was uneventful. However, at the end of surgery, about 60 minutes after the spinal blockade, the patient complained of repetitive muscle spasm in the absent amputated limb. The phantom spasm was distressful to the patient and was promptly treated with intravenous midazolam 5 mg. There was no recurrence of myoclonus and no sequelae.

args: A 48-year-old man with class II obesity and well-controlled hyperlipidemia was brought to the urgent care unit this morning because his left calf had become visibly larger and painful during the night. Four days earlier he was evaluated at a neighborhood clinic for influenza A confirmed by nasopharyngeal swab; he was advised to rest at home, and since that visit he has spent most of the time in bed, rising only to use the bathroom and to sip clear liquids. A step counter on his wristwatch logged fewer than 400 steps per day over the last three days, and his wife states he has needed help to the shower because of fatigue, but he has never been completely unable to move either leg. He denies traveling, wearing an orthopedic boot, splint, or cast, and has not experienced stroke, spinal cord injury, or limb weakness in the past. He also denies any recent operations, catheter placements, injections, arthroscopies, or endoscopies, and his most recent procedure was a screening colonoscopy two years ago. Yesterday afternoon, while shifting position in bed, he felt a dull ache in the posterior aspect of the left calf that was absent on the right. The discomfort intensified overnight, waking him twice when the blanket brushed against the skin, and by dawn he noticed the trouser leg on that side felt tight. He denies preceding trauma, insect bite, vigorous exercise, cellulitis, rash, or musculoskeletal strain. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, cough beyond his resolving influenza, hemoptysis, palpitations, fever since day one of his illness (afebrile the past 48 hours), abdominal pain, dysuria, or neurologic deficits. His medications are atorvastatin 20 mg nightly and over-the-counter acetaminophen 500 mg every six hours for myalgias; he has never used estrogen, testosterone, corticosteroids, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or anticoagulants. Family history is unremarkable for clotting disorders or premature vascular events, and he quit smoking a decade ago after a 15-pack-year exposure; he drinks beer on weekends but has had none this week. On arrival—prior to any medication—vital signs were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 126/80 mm Hg, heart rate 94 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air. He appeared fatigued but oriented and in mild discomfort when the left leg was dependent. Cardiac examination revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lung fields were clear with a few residual coarse breath sounds at the bases consistent with viral recovery. The abdomen was soft, nontender, and without organomegaly. Lower-extremity inspection showed obvious asymmetry of the calves while the thighs appeared symmetrical. A measuring tape placed 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity recorded a circumference of 40.2 cm on the left and 36.7 cm on the right, a difference of 3.5 cm. Mid-thigh girths were 52 cm bilaterally, confirming that swelling was limited to the distal portion of the limb rather than the entire extremity. The left calf skin was slightly taut and warmer than the contralateral side but retained normal coloration without erythema, brawny hyperpigmentation, or livedo. Firm thumb pressure over the anterior tibial crest produced no persistent indentation, and identical pressure on the right showed the same rapid rebound, indicating an absence of localized pitting. Gentle but systematic palpation along the medial and posterior course of the deep venous pathways—femoral triangle, adductor canal, popliteal fossa, and posterior calf compartments—elicited reproducible tenderness most pronounced midway between the popliteal crease and the Achilles insertion; no discrete cord was palpable. Passive dorsiflexion of the ankle and compression of the gastrocnemius increased the discomfort, whereas rotation of the knee and hip was painless, arguing against a joint source. Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial) were 2 + and symmetric, capillary refill was under two seconds, and sensation to light touch and pinprick remained intact in all toes. There were no distended superficial subcutaneous veins, no inguinal lymphadenopathy, no knee effusion, and no signs of lymphedema or ruptured Baker cyst. Baseline laboratories drawn before any anticoagulant consideration revealed hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, platelet count 232 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 6.8 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.96 mg/dL, sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.0 mmol/L, international normalized ratio 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 27 seconds. A plain radiograph of the tibia and fibula requested by triage showed soft-tissue prominence without fracture or gas. Given the acute unilateral calf enlargement exceeding 3 cm, the focal tenderness precisely along the expected deep venous track, the lack of pitting edema, the recent period of near-continuous bed rest surpassing three days, the absence of limb immobilization devices, recent surgery, malignancy, or a more credible orthopedic or infectious explanation, a duplex ultrasound of the left lower extremity has been ordered on an urgent basis before any therapeutic intervention.

args: A 27-year-old woman with no known chronic illnesses was brought to the emergency department by lifeguards after a shallow-water diving mishap at a public pool approximately twenty minutes earlier; witnesses report she struck her forehead on the concrete bottom before floating face-down for several seconds and was extricated on a spine board with a rigid collar applied. Prehospital personnel administered no analgesics, sedatives, or paralytics en route. On arrival she remains immobilized, supine, skin cool but well-perfused, with a thin trickle of blood from a superficial 1 cm laceration at the hairline. Initial vitals are blood pressure 128/76 mm Hg, heart rate 104 beats/min and regular, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min with shallow diaphragmatic effort, oxygen saturation 98 % on 6 L/min oxygen by face mask, and oral temperature 36.8 °C. Cardiac auscultation reveals normal S1/S2 without murmurs, and lungs are clear bilaterally, though chest rise is subdued. Inspection shows no chest wall bruising, abdomen is soft and non-distended, pelvis stable, and long bones without obvious deformity. Cranial nerves II–VII appear grossly intact: pupils are equal at 3 mm and briskly reactive, corneal and gag reflexes are present, and the mandible is midline. She lies with eyelids shut, lashes fluttering occasionally, but does not voluntarily raise them despite repeated verbal encouragement; a firm pinch to the trapezius elicits a brief parting of the lids, after which she tracks the examiner’s face for several seconds and states, in clear speech, “My name is ____; I dove into the pool; today is Friday; please help me,” demonstrating accurate orientation to person, place, and time. When asked to wiggle fingers or toes she expresses intent—“I’m trying, I can’t feel anything”—yet there is no visible contraction in upper or lower extremities. A nail-bed pressure to each hand and foot similarly produces no withdrawal, flexion, or extension, and deep tendon reflexes at biceps, triceps, patellae, and Achilles are absent. Rectal tone is flaccid. Sensory testing reveals no appreciation of pinprick or light touch below the clavicles, while facial sensation remains intact. There is no diaphragmatic paradox, but accessory muscle use is noted. The collar obscures evaluation of the neck; however, gentle palpation of the shoulders provokes discomfort reported verbally. Capillary blood glucose is 109 mg/dL. She denies headache, visual changes, or prior neurologic disease and confirms she last ate four hours ago; medications include only a daily multivitamin, and she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and denies illicit substance use. No family members are present yet. Skin survey identifies scattered erythematous abrasions along the anterior shins consistent with pool-bottom scrape, without active bleeding. Bilateral radial and dorsalis pedis pulses are 2+. Portable anterior-posterior chest radiograph obtained immediately at bedside shows intact lung fields and no mediastinal widening; focused assessment with sonography for trauma is negative for free fluid. While preparations are made for emergent computed tomography of the brain, cervical spine, and entire spine, the patient remains conversational though intermittently drowsy; between painful stimuli she allows her lids to drift closed and appears to doze, breathing spontaneously without stridor. No intravenous analgesia has been given, and she is fasting pending imaging. Neurosurgery and trauma teams have been paged with concern for a high cervical spinal cord injury producing complete motor paralysis below the level of lesion. Continuous pulse oximetry and end-tidal capnography have been initiated given the risk of evolving respiratory compromise.

args: A 22-year-old woman with no past medical history, no chronic medication use, and up-to-date routine vaccinations was transported to the critical care receiving area twenty minutes after being pulled from a freshwater quarry where she had been swimming with classmates during a midsummer outing; witnesses reported that she inhaled water during a brief struggle to stay afloat and was unconscious for “less than a minute” before lifeguards extricated her. Paramedics arrived eight minutes after the call and documented that on ambient air her pulse oximetry registered in the low seventies, prompting placement of a tightly-fitting Venturi mask delivering 50 % inspired oxygen; during the ten-minute ride she received no intravenous fluids, no bronchodilators, no antimicrobials, no vasopressors, and no opioids, and her airway remained patent without adjuncts or positive-pressure support. Upon stretcher entry to the resuscitation bay—and before any hospital-initiated therapy—vital signs were captured: tympanic temperature 38.2 °C, regular but rapid pulse 118 beats per minute confirmed on three-lead telemetry, observed respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute with moderate accessory muscle recruitment, and a non-invasive blood pressure that cycled consistently at 122⁄70 mm Hg on both arms. She opened her eyes to spoken name, answered most orientation questions but was unsure of the exact date, and followed two-step commands, establishing a Glasgow Coma Score of 14 without sedatives, ethanol, or head trauma. Skin examination revealed flushed cheeks, warm extremities, brisk capillary refill, and no peripheral cyanosis or edema. Jugular venous pulsations were not distended at 30 degrees, carotid upstrokes were sharp, and distal pulses were bounding. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a loud S1, physiologic split S2, and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Breath sounds were coarse bilaterally with diffuse crackles more pronounced over the mid-zones, and percussion was mildly dull at the bases, consistent with fluid-laden alveoli rather than pleural effusion. The abdomen was flat, nondistended, and nontender with active bowel sounds; no hepatosplenomegaly was appreciated. There was no costovertebral angle tenderness. Extremities displayed full range of motion without crepitus or deformity, and neurologic screen showed equal pupils, symmetric facial movement, and 5⁄5 strength in all limbs. Seven minutes after arrival, while she remained on the unchanged 50 % oxygen source, a right radial arterial sample produced a pH of 7.43, PaCO₂ 37 mm Hg, and an analyzer-calculated alveolar-to-arterial oxygen gradient of 480 mm Hg, indicative of severe impairment of oxygen transfer despite near-normal ventilation. Capnography showed an end-tidal CO₂ of 36 mm Hg, correlating with the arterial value. Simultaneous point-of-care basic metabolic panel revealed sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, glucose 102 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 12 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.8 mg/dL—values matching those from a collegiate sports physical filed six months earlier, suggesting intact renal function without acute injury. A rapid complete blood count returned a white-cell count of 17 050 cells/mm³ with neutrophil predominance, hematocrit 42 %, hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL, and platelets 244 000/µL. Venous lactate measured 2.4 mmol/L. Portable anteroposterior chest radiography obtained before any diuretics, antibiotics, or positive-pressure ventilation demonstrated bilateral patchy infiltrates with ill-defined air bronchograms, no pleural fluid, and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. Point-of-care lung ultrasound corroborated diffuse B-lines throughout both fields without discrete consolidation or pneumothorax. Focused transthoracic echocardiography revealed a small, vigorously contracting left ventricle with an estimated ejection fraction over 65 % and an inferior vena cava measuring 1.4 cm that collapsed completely with inspiration, findings consonant with euvolemia rather than fluid overload. Twelve-lead electrocardiography printed during the arterial draw showed sinus tachycardia at 116 beats per minute, PR interval 150 ms, QRS duration 88 ms, QTc 420 ms, and no acute ischemic changes. A bladder scan indicated 90 mL of urine, and a straight catheter placed for accurate output monitoring drained 25 mL of clear yellow urine over the subsequent ten minutes. Importantly, from the moment of extraction from the water through handoff to the intensive-care team—a span of roughly forty minutes—the patient had not received intravenous crystalloid, antimicrobial agents, bronchodilators, corticosteroids, analgesics, vasopressors, or diuretics; the only intervention in place remained the Venturi mask delivering 50 % oxygen. Thus, the contemporaneous physiologic and laboratory measurements—temperature 38.2 °C, systolic pressure 122 mm Hg, diastolic pressure 70 mm Hg, heart rate 118 beats per minute, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, arterial pH 7.43, serum sodium 140 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, hematocrit 42 %, white blood cell count 17 050 /mm³, inspired oxygen fraction 50 %, and an alveolar-to-arterial gradient of 480 mm Hg—represent her untreated presentation for suspected aspiration-related acute lung injury manifesting as moderate fever, leukocytosis, and significant hypoxemia.
